description: The foundation of a Mexican accent: five pure, short, constant vowels — [a e i o u] — that never glide the way English vowels do. 'a' as in 'father', 'e' as in 'bet', 'i' as in 'machine', 'o' as in 'or', 'u' as in 'flute'. They keep the same crisp value stressed or unstressed: the o in 'como' is a clean [o] both times, never the mushy 'uh' English gives unstressed syllables. Get the vowels clean and short and you're already halfway to sounding Mexican rather than foreign.

minimalPairs.note: both o's/a's stay pure and equal — no reduction

description: The defining Latin American trait: **seseo**. The letters c (before e/i), z, and s are ALL pronounced [s] — there is no Castilian 'th' [θ]. So 'cinco', 'zapato', 'gracias', 'cerveza' all use plain [s]: SEEN-ko, sa-PA-to, GRA-syas, ser-VE-sa. This is exactly the accent Azure's es-MX model expects; producing the Spanish [θ] would sound out of place in Mexico and can confuse pronunciation scoring. Bonus: 'caza' (hunt) and 'casa' (house) are homophones here — both [ˈka.sa].

minimalPairs.note: 'to cook' and 'to sew' are homophones under seseo
the c/z and the s are the identical [s] sound

description: **Yeísmo**: 'll' and 'y' are pronounced the same, roughly English 'y' in 'yes' — [ʝ]. So 'pollo' = PO-yo, 'calle' = KA-ye, 'llave' = YA-ve, 'ella' = E-ya. In much of central Mexico it's a soft [ʝ]; it can strengthen toward a 'j' sound in some speakers/positions, but the soft version is safe and standard. Do NOT pronounce ll as English 'l' + 'l' ('pol-lo'). The Argentine 'sh' sound (SHO for 'yo') is a different dialect — not Mexican.

The letter j, and g before e/i, are the throaty [x] — a raspy 'h' made at the back of the throat, stronger than English h: 'jamón' [xa.ˈmon], 'gente' [ˈxen.te], 'trabajo' [tɾa.ˈβa.xo], 'México' [ˈme.xi.ko]. In Mexico it's often softer than the harsh Castilian jota but still clearly back-of-throat, not a plain English 'h'. This is why 'México', 'Oaxaca', and 'Xalapa' sound like 'MEH-hee-ko', 'wa-HA-ka', 'ha-LA-pa' — that x is the [x] sound.

minimalPairs.note: hard [g] before a vs soft [x] before e

description: g is hard [g] before a, o, u ('gato', 'gustar') and between vowels softens to a light [ɣ] ('lago', 'agua'). To keep it hard before e/i, Spanish inserts a silent u: 'guerra' [ˈge.ra], 'guitarra' [gi.ˈta.ra] — the u is NOT pronounced. When that u DOES need to sound (before e/i), it gets a dieresis: 'pingüino', 'vergüenza'. Before a/o the u sounds normally: 'agua', 'guapo' have a real w-glide.

minimalPairs.note: silent u (war) vs dieresis ü pronounced (blonde woman)
hard [g] at start vs soft [ɣ] between vowels

description: A single r between vowels is a quick **tap** [ɾ] — the tongue flicks the ridge behind the teeth once, exactly like the American English 'tt' in 'butter' or 'dd' in 'ladder'. 'pero' [ˈpe.ɾo], 'cara', 'para', 'toro'. Anglophones already make this sound in fast English — the trick is using it deliberately for Spanish single r rather than the English bunched 'r'.

The double **rr**, and any r at the start of a word, is the rolled **trill** [r] — multiple rapid taps, the tongue vibrating against the ridge: 'perro' [ˈpe.ro], 'rojo', 'ratón', 'carro'. This contrasts meaningfully with the single tap: 'pero' (but) vs 'perro' (dog), 'caro' (expensive) vs 'carro' (car). It's the hardest sound for many learners — practice from a 'd' or the tap, adding air; approximating with a strong tap is understood while you build the trill.

minimalPairs.note: trill (dog) vs tap (but) — the classic Spanish minimal pair

description: b and v are pronounced **identically** in Spanish — there is no English 'v' (teeth-on-lip) sound. Both are [b] at the start of an utterance or after m/n ('bueno', 'vamos', 'invierno'), and both soften to a gentle [β] (lips barely touching, air passing) between vowels ('haba', 'lava', 'nueve'). So 'baca' and 'vaca' sound the same. Spelling keeps them apart on paper only; Mexicans clarify by saying 'b grande / b de burro' vs 'v chica / v de vaca'.

minimalPairs.note: hard [b] at start vs soft [β] between vowels

description: d is a full [d] at the start or after n/l ('dónde', 'falda'), but between vowels and at the end of words it softens to [ð] — the soft 'th' of English 'this': 'nada' [ˈna.ða], 'todo', 'ciudad'. Final -d is very soft, often barely there: 'usted' can sound like 'usté', 'Madrid' like 'Madrí'. Pronouncing every d as a hard English d ('NA-da' with a punchy d) is a giveaway; let the intervocalic ones melt.

minimalPairs.note: hard [d] at start, soft [ð] between vowels

description: ñ is the palatal nasal [ɲ], the 'ny' in 'canyon' — one sound, tongue flat against the roof of the mouth: 'año' [ˈa.ɲo], 'niño', 'España', 'mañana', 'señor'. It is a separate letter from n, and the difference is not cosmetic: 'año' (year) vs 'ano' (anus), 'campaña' (campaign) vs 'campana' (bell). Never pronounce it as plain n.

minimalPairs.note: 'year' vs 'anus' — the tilde is not optional

description: ch is [tʃ], the 'ch' in English 'church' — one crisp sound: 'chile' [ˈtʃi.le], 'mucho', 'noche', 'ocho', 'chido'. Straightforward for English speakers; just keep it consistent and don't soften it to 'sh'.

The letter h is **always silent** in Spanish — it makes no sound at all: 'hola' = OH-la, 'hombre' = OM-bre, 'ahora' = a-O-ra, 'huevo' = WE-vo, 'hijo' = EE-ho. Never give it the English breathy h. (The throaty 'h' sound you DO hear in Spanish is written j or g, not h — see the [x] rule.) So 'hola' and 'ola' (wave) are homophones.

The trickiest letter — x has four values in Mexico, and place names drive it. Usually [ks] in loanwords ('taxi', 'éxito'). But in many Indigenous-derived words it's the [x] jota sound: 'México' [ˈme.xi.ko], 'Oaxaca' [wa.ˈxa.ka], 'Xalapa' [xa.ˈla.pa]. In others it's [s]: 'Xochimilco' [so.tʃi.ˈmil.ko]. And in a few it keeps an old [ʃ] 'sh': 'Xola', 'mixiote'. There's no rule — these are memorized per word. The critical one: 'México' and 'mexicano' use [x], never [ks].

qu is plain [k] with a totally silent u: 'que' [ke], 'quién' [kjen], 'aquí' [a.ˈki], 'queso' [ˈke.so] — never the English 'kw'. The [k] sound is spelled 'c' before a/o/u (casa, como, cuatro) and 'qu' before e/i (queso, quince). So c and qu never clash; it's one sound, two spellings driven by the following vowel.

minimalPairs.note: qu = [k] with silent u vs cu = real [kw]

description: The letter y is a consonant [ʝ] before a vowel ('yo', 'ya', 'ayer' — see yeísmo) but a vowel [i] when it stands alone as the word 'y' (and) or ends a word: 'y' = [i], 'hoy' [oj], 'muy' [mwi], 'estoy' [es.ˈtoj]. So the same letter is a consonant or a vowel depending on position — an easy trap when 'y' (and) links two words: 'café y pan' is just [i] gluing them.

minimalPairs.note: final y = vowel [i]; y before vowel = [ʝ]

description: Spanish stress is predictable by two rules: (1) words ending in a **vowel, n, or s** are stressed on the **second-to-last** syllable — CA-sa, CO-men, ta-COS, ha-BLA-mos. (2) words ending in **any other consonant** are stressed on the **last** syllable — ha-BLAR, ciu-DAD, es-pa-ÑOL, re-LOJ. That's it — most words follow these two rules with no accent mark needed. Stress is meaningful: 'hablo' (I speak) vs 'habló' (he spoke), 'papa' (potato) vs 'papá' (dad).

The acute accent (tilde) does one main job: mark the stressed syllable when a word **breaks** the two default rules. 'café' (ends in vowel but stressed last), 'árbol' (ends in consonant but stressed second-to-last), 'teléfono', 'México', 'sábado'. It also distinguishes identical-looking words (diacritic accent): 'sí' (yes) / 'si' (if), 'tú' (you) / 'tu' (your), 'él' (he) / 'el' (the), 'más' (more) / 'mas' (but), and every question word (qué, dónde, cómo). If you can hear the stress, the accent mark tells you it lands somewhere unexpected.

minimalPairs.note: same sound; accent marks the pronoun 'he' vs article 'the'
the accent shows stress falls on the unexpected first syllable

description: When a weak vowel (i, u) meets a strong one (a, e, o), they glide together into one syllable — a diphthong: 'bien' [bjen], 'bueno' [ˈbwe.no], 'seis' [sejs], 'agua' [ˈa.ɣwa], 'ciudad' (i+u), 'aire'. Keep them smooth and single-syllable; don't break them into two beats. An accent on the weak vowel breaks the diphthong into separate syllables: 'día' [ˈdi.a] (two syllables), 'país' [pa.ˈis], 'río'. This is why 'día' has an accent — it splits what would otherwise be one syllable.

minimalPairs.note: diphthong 'toward' vs broken 'I did/made' — the accent splits it

description: Spanish runs words together in a smooth stream, especially where one word ends and the next begins with a vowel — the vowels blend and the boundary vanishes: 'está aquí' → [es.ta.ˈki], 'mi hermano' → [mjeɾ.ˈma.no], 'la escuela' → [la.es.ˈkwe.la]. This 'sinalefa' is why fast Mexican Spanish sounds like one long word to beginners. Leaning into it — not pausing between words — makes you sound fluent; hearing it is the key to understanding native speed.

minimalPairs.note: vowels link across the word boundary

description: Because yes/no questions keep statement word order, **intonation does the work**: the voice rises at the end of a yes/no question — '¿Hablas español?↗' — where a statement falls. Information questions (with qué, dónde…) fall like statements, the question word carrying the load. The opening ¿ is your visual cue to start the question melody early. Mexican Spanish also has a gentle, somewhat sing-song sentence rhythm; flattening everything into English stress-timing is a bigger accent tell than any single sound.

minimalPairs.note: same words; rising vs falling pitch makes it a question
intonation alone marks the yes/no question
